network.overall_strategy: This network prioritizes the literature-supported cyanosulfidic synthesis of an arginine alpha-aminonitrile precursor from acetylene, HCN, NH3, H2S, cyanamide, and UV-driven Cu photoredox chemistry in shallow surface settings, then converts that precursor to free arginine by hydrolysis in prebiotic aqueous environments. Hydrothermal chemistry is used conservatively and literature-consistently to supply reduced feedstocks and shared hubs such as formate, acetate, pyruvate, ammonia, and sulfide under Fe-S mineral catalysis, which then feed surface photoredox chemistry. A secondary, explicitly downgraded route follows the reported abiotic guanidination of ornithine-containing peptides or free ornithine by cyanamide in aqueous solution, reflecting the literature view that arginine may have entered chemical evolution as a post-synthetic modification rather than as a primary free monomer. The ten pathways below are distinct traversals through one connected bipartite network and mostly converge on the best-supported arginine alpha-aminonitrile hub before final hydrolysis to arginine.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, anoxic, H2-rich serpentinization setting

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface mediated reduction of CO2 by H2 on Fe-S phases to give formate.
reactions.reasoning: Vent Fe-S minerals plausibly reduce CO2 to simple C1 organics under alkaline hydrothermal conditions, supplying reduced carbon to downstream prebiotic networks; this is consistent with iron-sulfur world and vent carbon-fixation studies (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Russell & Martin, 2004; Preiner et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal homologation from formate to acetate

conditions.other: anoxic vent pores with redox and pH gradients

reactions.mechanism: Further reduction and C-C bond formation on Fe-bearing mineral surfaces generates acetate from reduced C1 feedstocks.
reactions.reasoning: Acetate formation from reduced carbon species under hydrothermal Fe-mineral catalysis is a core component of vent metabolism-first chemistry and provides a connected reduced-carbon pool feeding later synthesis (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Muchowska et al., 2019; Preiner et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate formation from reduced carbon pool

conditions.other: mild alkaline hydrothermal regime; included conservatively as vent-supported alpha-keto acid generation

reactions.mechanism: Successive reduction and carboxylation/homologation on iron minerals furnishes the alpha-keto acid pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate is reported as plausibly formed in hydrothermal vent simulations and is central to vent protometabolism, though this step is less directly established than the cyanosulfidic arginine route and is therefore used only as a feedstock-connecting node rather than a primary arginine-forming step (Muchowska et al., 2019; Preiner et al., 2020; Varma et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine-equivalent ammonia pool

conditions.other: anoxic vent microcompartments; represented as sustaining shared NH3/amino donor pool rather than introducing a separate alanine node

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-assisted reductive amination chemistry shows that vent-generated keto acids can engage ammonia to generate amino-acid chemistry, reinforcing NH3-linked network connectivity.
reactions.reasoning: The report specifically notes pyruvate reductive amination under vent-like conditions; here it is incorporated in a compressed way to connect vent alpha-keto acid chemistry with downstream amination potential without overclaiming unsupported ornithine synthesis (Barge et al., 2019; Muchowska et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal sulfide and ammonia supply from vent serpentinization and mineral nitrogen chemistry

conditions.other: serpentinization-influenced vent fluids; anoxic, reduced iron abundant

reactions.mechanism: Reduced hydrothermal systems furnish sulfide-rich, ammonia-compatible fluids and support mineral-mediated nitrogen reduction chemistry that populates the NH3/H2S pool.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research emphasizes serpentinization-generated reducing power, Fe-S mineral catalysis, and the importance of NH3 and H2S in vent-to-surface chemistry; this aggregated sourcing step is used to connect hydrothermal and surface subsystems while staying within the geochemical framework (Russell & Martin, 2004; Martin et al., 2008; Preiner et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Surface Cu-photoredox coupling of acetylene and HCN to acrylonitrile

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I) photoredox cycling in sulfide-rich, UV-irradiated shallow surface setting
conditions.other: anoxic, UV-exposed shallow ponds or intertidal alkaline vent margins; mineral agents provide surface localization while Cu(I) is the literature-specific catalytic metal

reactions.mechanism: Cu(I)-mediated C-C cross-coupling between acetylene and HCN forms acrylonitrile, establishing the three-carbon nitrile backbone.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research identifies Cu(I)-catalyzed coupling of acetylene and HCN to acrylonitrile as the entry step to the Sutherland arginine pathway; shallow photoredox environments are specifically emphasized (Patel et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Surface ammonia addition to acrylonitrile

conditions.other: anoxic aqueous pools with evaporative concentration

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of ammonia across the activated nitrile-containing alkene yields beta-aminopropionitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Ammonia addition to acrylonitrile to give beta-aminopropionitrile is explicitly described in the literature-supported arginine sequence and provides the immediate substrate for guanidinylation (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface guanidinylation of beta-aminopropionitrile by cyanamide

conditions.catalyst: cyanamide reagent in concentrated aqueous phase on mineral surfaces
conditions.other: neutral to mildly alkaline, anoxic; reaction known to be incomplete and mixture-forming

reactions.mechanism: The amino group attacks cyanamide, forming a guanidinated nitrile derivative that seeds the arginine side chain.
reactions.reasoning: The report states that beta-aminopropionitrile reacts with cyanamide to form a guanidinated derivative, albeit inefficiently, and that this mixed pool is then productively processed by photoredox chemistry (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface photoredox trapping to hemiaminal 37

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox with H2S under UV irradiation
conditions.other: oxygen-free, UV-driven; residual cyanamide traps the aldehyde as a stable hemiaminal

reactions.mechanism: Photoreduction in the presence of sulfide generates the corresponding aldehyde level, which is efficiently trapped by cyanamide as the stable hemiaminal 37.
reactions.reasoning: The key literature discovery is the extraordinarily efficient formation of a stable hemiaminal from beta-aminopropionaldehyde and residual cyanamide during UV/H2S/Cu photoredox processing; this trapping step is central to selectivity (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface dark cyanohydrin formation from hemiaminal 37

conditions.catalyst: mineral-assisted concentration in dark aqueous phase
conditions.other: dark reaction following photochemical step; anoxic

reactions.mechanism: HCN addition to the trapped aldehydic center gives the homologated cyanohydrin intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: The report explicitly states that after hemiaminal formation, a dark reaction with HCN converts the intermediate into a cyanohydrin as part of a Kiliani-Fischer-like homologation sequence (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface sulfidolysis of cyanohydrin to alpha-hydroxythioamide
reactions.mechanism: Sulfide substitutes into the cyanohydrin-derived intermediate to generate an alpha-hydroxythioamide.
reactions.reasoning: Sulfidolysis of the cyanohydrin to the alpha-hydroxythioamide is directly described in the cyanosulfidic arginine pathway and depends on H2S-rich conditions (Patel et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Surface UV deoxygenation of alpha-hydroxythioamide to thioamide

conditions.catalyst: UV irradiation with Cu photoredox-compatible mineral setting

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical deoxygenation removes the alpha-hydroxyl functionality, yielding the thioamide.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research explicitly identifies UV irradiation of the alpha-hydroxythioamide as the deoxygenation step producing a thioamide in the homologation sequence (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface photoreduction and final amination to arginine alpha-aminonitrile

conditions.catalyst: UV-driven Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox with H2S, followed by NH3 and HCN incorporation

reactions.mechanism: Photoreduction of the thioamide followed by cyanation and amination furnishes the alpha-aminonitrile precursor of arginine.
reactions.reasoning: This is the culmination of the Patel et al. cyanosulfidic sequence, producing the arginine alpha-aminonitrile directly from the thioamide under UV/H2S/Cu-photoredox conditions with NH3 and HCN (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Biochemical/prebiotic hydrolysis of arginine alpha-aminonitrile to arginine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis during geothermal cycling or standing pools; slower, potentially stepwise nitrile hydration/hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Nitrile hydrolysis converts the alpha-aminonitrile to the corresponding amino acid, arginine.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of amino nitriles to amino acids is a standard completion step in Strecker-type and cyanosulfidic amino acid synthesis; while the report emphasizes the aminonitrile precursor more strongly than explicit free-arginine hydrolysis conditions, this conversion is the minimal necessary finishing step to reach the target and is included as a cautious connection (Strecker, 1850; Patel et al., 2015; Leman et al., 2004).

reactions.name: Surface HCN-derived formation of ornithine as a secondary basic amino acid pool

conditions.catalyst: concentration on mineral surfaces in HCN-rich aqueous pools
conditions.other: explicitly secondary/speculative compression of HCN-solution and spark-derived ornithine chemistry; anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Networked HCN chemistry and hydrolysis generate a low-yield ornithine pool used only as a secondary precursor branch.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research states that ornithine is more prebiotically accessible than arginine and has been identified in HCN solutions and spark-discharge experiments, whereas arginine is absent. Because the exact multi-step route is not fully detailed in the provided report, this node is included only as a secondary, explicitly lower-confidence feeder consistent with the literature trend rather than as a primary pathway (Parker et al., 2011; Cleaves et al., 2008; Longo et al., 2013).

reactions.name: Biochemical peptide bond formation to ornithine-containing dipeptide

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling in prebiotic pools or mineral interfaces; condensation-dehydration regime

reactions.mechanism: Dry-down condensation yields short peptides that incorporate ornithine side chains.
reactions.reasoning: The report emphasizes prebiotic dry-down peptide formation and the importance of ornithine-containing early statistical polypeptides as likely precursors to later arginine-bearing peptides (Forsythe et al., 2015; Frenkel-Pinter et al., 2020; Longo et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Biochemical post-synthetic guanidination of free ornithine by cyanamide

conditions.other: room temperature, aqueous solution, neutral to slightly alkaline pH

reactions.mechanism: The side-chain primary amine of ornithine attacks cyanamide, producing the guanidino side chain of arginine.
reactions.reasoning: Abiotic guanidination of ornithine by cyanamide in aqueous solution at room temperature and near-neutral to mildly alkaline pH is directly supported by the supplied literature and is the strongest secondary route to arginine outside the main cyanosulfidic aminonitrile pathway (Longo et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Biochemical post-synthetic guanidination of ornithine-containing peptide

conditions.other: room temperature, aqueous solution, neutral to slightly alkaline pH; statistical side-chain conversion

reactions.mechanism: Peptide-bound ornithine side chains undergo selective guanidination by cyanamide to generate peptide-bound arginine residues.
reactions.reasoning: The literature explicitly reports statistical conversion of ornithine side chains in peptides to arginine by cyanamide, improving folding and nucleic-acid binding and supporting a post-synthetic entry of arginine into early peptides (Longo et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolytic release of arginine from arginine-containing peptide

conditions.other: geothermal cycling or prolonged aqueous exposure; included as a necessary but less directly evidenced release step

reactions.mechanism: Peptide bond hydrolysis liberates free arginine from an arginine-containing short peptide.
reactions.reasoning: The report focuses on peptide guanidination as the key supported phenomenon and does not establish peptide hydrolysis as a dominant productive route to free arginine; this step is therefore explicitly speculative but chemically necessary to connect peptide-bound arginine to the free target within one integrated network (Longo et al., 2020; Frenkel-Pinter et al., 2020).

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic route from atmospheric acetylene
pathways.description: The primary literature-supported surface photoredox route beginning from acetylene and HCN, proceeding through acrylonitrile, beta-aminopropionitrile, guanidinated derivative, hemiaminal 37, cyanohydrin, alpha-hydroxythioamide, thioamide, arginine alpha-aminonitrile, and final hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal sulfide-assisted cyanosulfidic route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal vents furnish the H2S/NH3 pool that is transported to shallow photoredox settings, where the canonical cyanosulfidic route produces arginine.

pathways.name: Vent formate-to-ornithine secondary branch
pathways.description: Reduced carbon from hydrothermal CO2 reduction contributes to a surface HCN chemistry pool that yields ornithine at low confidence, followed by direct cyanamide guanidination to arginine.

pathways.name: Vent acetate-enhanced ornithine branch
pathways.description: Hydrothermal homologation to acetate augments surface reduced-carbon availability feeding a secondary ornithine pool, then cyanamide converts ornithine to arginine.

pathways.name: Vent pyruvate-linked ornithine branch
pathways.description: A conservative hydrothermal alpha-keto acid route is connected to HCN-derived surface ornithine formation and then direct guanidination to arginine.

pathways.name: Peptide-first ornithine guanidination route
pathways.description: Secondary ornithine is incorporated into a short peptide during wet-dry cycling and then selectively guanidinated by cyanamide to form an arginine-containing peptide, followed by hydrolytic release of free arginine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-fed peptide-first route
pathways.description: Vent-derived reduced carbon and nitrogen feed surface ornithine formation, after which peptide assembly and post-synthetic cyanamide guanidination yield peptide-bound arginine and then free arginine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface canonical route via reduced carbon supply
pathways.description: Vent-generated formate and acetate populate the shared feedstock pool while shallow-surface photoredox chemistry executes the established arginine alpha-aminonitrile sequence.

pathways.name: Ammonia-rich canonical cyanosulfidic route
pathways.description: A route emphasizing hydrothermal NH3 delivery into the surface acrylonitrile-to-aminonitrile sequence, capturing convergence at beta-aminopropionitrile and arginine alpha-aminonitrile.

pathways.name: Mixed canonical and ornithine-modification convergence network
pathways.description: A systems-chemistry pathway in which the environment simultaneously sustains the direct aminonitrile route to arginine and the secondary ornithine-to-arginine route, both converging on the same final product.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strongest pathway in the network. It closely matches the literature-supported Patel/Sutherland-style cyanosulfidic arginine precursor sequence from acetylene, HCN, NH3, H2S, cyanamide, Cu photoredox, and UV, followed by hydrolysis of the alpha-aminonitrile to arginine. The chemistry is selective and internally coherent. Minor uncertainty remains in the last hydrolysis step because full free-arginine release under broadly plausible prebiotic conditions is less directly established than formation of the aminonitrile precursor.
suggestions: Explicitly specify Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox and UV conditions in the surface reactions if absent in the full network record.
Clarify hydrolysis conditions for conversion of arginine alpha-aminonitrile to free arginine, including pH and temperature window compatible with amino acid survival.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The main cyanosulfidic segment is strong, but the hydrothermal feeder step is weakly defined. Vent generation of H2S is plausible, yet coupling this in the same reaction with ammonia production from CO2 and H2 is not literature-secure and appears overcompressed. Still, as a supply-augmented version of the canonical route, the overall pathway remains acceptable.
suggestions: Split hydrothermal sulfide generation and ammonia generation into separate reactions with explicit mineral and nitrogen-source logic.
Avoid implying direct NH3 formation from CO2 and H2 without a defensible fixed-nitrogen precursor.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This branch depends on a poorly supported single-step formation of ornithine from HCN, ammonia, and formate. The literature supports ornithine as more prebiotically accessible than arginine, but not this compressed route. The subsequent cyanamide guanidination of ornithine is literature-aligned, though better established for side-chain modification in peptides than for selective bulk synthesis of free arginine.
suggestions: Replace the one-step ornithine formation with a multistep nitrile/homologation sequence or clearly label it as speculative.
Downgrade the confidence of direct free-ornithine guanidination relative to peptide-side-chain guanidination.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The acetate-augmented branch is topologically connected but chemically underdeveloped. Vent formate-to-acetate chemistry is plausible on Fe-S minerals, yet the added value of acetate is not mechanistically integrated into ornithine synthesis. The bottleneck remains the unsupported direct ornithine-forming step.
suggestions: Show exactly how acetate contributes carbon skeleton growth to ornithine instead of merely including it upstream.
Use experimentally grounded intermediates for ornithine synthesis.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This branch adds pyruvate, which is a metabolically relevant hydrothermal hub, but the path to ornithine still lacks a credible mechanistic bridge. The pyruvate formation step is plausible in broad vent chemistry terms, but not strongly tied to the subsequent ornithine branch as drawn.
suggestions: Use pyruvate in a concrete sequence toward a validated ornithine precursor rather than as a disconnected feeder.
Reduce reliance on speculative one-step backbone assembly.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The peptide-first guanidination concept aligns well with the literature claim that arginine may have emerged via post-synthetic modification of ornithine residues. Guanidination of ornithine-containing peptides by cyanamide is one of the most literature-aligned secondary routes here. However, the pathway remains held back by the weakly supported source of free ornithine and some uncertainty in hydrolytic release of free arginine from the modified dipeptide.
suggestions: Provide a more defensible prebiotic route to ornithine.
If the goal is arginine in peptides rather than free arginine, stop at the guanidinated peptide stage to improve credibility.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This hydrothermal-fed peptide-first route is conceptually reasonable from a systems perspective, but the actual chemistry remains dominated by the speculative ornithine source. The later peptide guanidination step is much stronger than the early branch chemistry.
suggestions: Rebuild the ornithine supply branch from experimentally supported nitrile intermediates.
Distinguish peptide modification chemistry from free amino acid synthesis more clearly.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good whole-system pathway showing hydrothermal reduced-carbon supply feeding a strong surface cyanosulfidic route and ending in prebiotic hydrolysis. The vent chemistry is somewhat generic and compressed, but it does not fatally undermine the main path because the decisive transformations are literature-consistent surface reactions.
suggestions: Strengthen hydrothermal feed reactions with clearer literature-supported stoichiometric and mineral detail.
Keep hydrothermal chemistry as feedstock generation rather than implying direct arginine-specific synthesis at vents.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is another acceptable variant of the canonical route, but it inherits the same weakness regarding hydrothermal ammonia sourcing. The core arginine precursor sequence remains strong and target-achieving.
suggestions: Use a more realistic fixed-nitrogen source for ammonia availability.
Avoid overclaiming direct hydrothermal nitrogen reduction unless supported by specific cited mineral chemistry.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This mixed pathway benefits from convergence: a highly credible canonical cyanosulfidic route plus a weaker ornithine-modification branch. Because the strong branch is present, the pathway as a whole still reaches an acceptable confidence level, but the network would be better if the ornithine branch were explicitly marked speculative.
suggestions: Separate high-confidence canonical chemistry from exploratory ornithine chemistry in confidence annotations.
Provide literature support or downgrade for direct ornithine formation.

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a well-connected synthesis network with good topological structure and a strong central route to arginine. Its main strength is close alignment with the literature-supported cyanosulfidic synthesis of arginine alpha-aminonitrile from acetylene, HCN, NH3, H2S, cyanamide, and Cu/UV photoredox chemistry, followed by hydrolysis to arginine. The network also appropriately reflects the literature idea that ornithine-to-arginine conversion may have occurred as a post-synthetic modification, especially in peptides. Environmental interconnection is good overall: hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced feedstocks and sulfur/ammonia pools, surface chemistry performs the decisive photoredox homologation, and biochemical/prebiotic steps perform hydrolysis and peptide chemistry. However, several hydrothermal and secondary-branch reactions are overcompressed or weakly justified. In particular, rxn_005 unrealistically bundles sulfide and ammonia supply from CO2 and H2, rxn_004 is chemically problematic because pyruvate reductive amination should not regenerate ammonia as sole output, and rxn_015 is an inadequately supported one-step ornithine synthesis. The network therefore succeeds because its canonical branch is strong, not because every branch is equally plausible. Literature alignment is good for the main route and moderate for the auxiliary branches. One formal concern is that the provided summary does not show the required mineral agents for each hydrothermal and surface reaction; if those are absent in the full JSON, that would be a schema-level deficiency. Overall, this is an approve-with-reservations network driven by one excellent pathway embedded in a partially speculative broader system.
